query: If I had a wind asset in ERCOT West and a solar asset with the same capacity in CAISO SP15 zone in 2024, which one would make me the most merchant revenue assuming real time market prices only and no curtailment in both regions. Use revenue numbers in $/MWh for the comparison
final_answer:
Now I have all the data needed. Here is the complete analysis.

---

## Result: The ERCOT West wind asset generates **~5.7× more merchant revenue** than the CAISO SP15 solar asset

### Data Sources & Methodology

| Component | Data Source | Details |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **ERCOT West RT LMP** | ERCOT database (`ercot_lmp` table) | Real-time LMPs at West Hub, hourly, 2024 (8,783 hours) |
| **CAISO SP15 RT LMP** | GridStatus `caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min` | Real-time 15-min LMPs at `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` trading hub, 2024 (35,129 intervals → hourly averaged) |
| **Wind Profile** | Renewables.ninja | McCamey, TX (31.13°N, -102.22°W), Vestas V90 2000, 80m hub height, 2024 |
| **Solar Profile** | Renewables.ninja | Palm Springs, CA (33.82°N, -116.54°W), fixed 35° tilt, 180° azimuth, 10% system losses, 2024 |

### Revenue Calculation

Merchant revenue = **Hourly LMP × Hourly Generation (CF)**, summed over all 8,784 hours of 2024, no curtailment assumed.

| Metric | **Wind (ERCOT West)** | **Solar (CAISO SP15)** |
|--------|:---------------------:|:----------------------:|
| Annual Capacity Factor | **42.6%** | **21.5%** |
| Total Generation (MWh/MW) | 3,735 | 1,891 |
| Simple Avg RT LMP | **$27.44/MWh** | **$31.00/MWh** |
| **Revenue-Weighted Avg LMP** | **$20.52/MWh** | **$7.11/MWh** |
| **Total Merchant Revenue/MW-year** | **$76,644** | **$13,445** |

### Why the Wind Asset Wins by Such a Large Margin

**1. Higher capacity factor (42.6% vs 21.5%)** — West Texas wind produces ~2× more MWh per MW of capacity than South California solar.

**2. Severe solar price cannibalization in SP15** — The CAISO "duck curve" depresses RT prices precisely when solar generates. During UTC hours 16–23 (roughly California's peak sunlight), the average SP15 RT LMP is just **$8.73/MWh** (compared to an all-hours average of $31.00/MWh). Over 1,183 hours in 2024, SP15 prices went **negative** while the solar asset was generating, destroying ~$16,258/MW in revenue.

**3. Wind captures higher-priced hours** — ERCOT West wind tends to blow more at night (when prices are moderate) and during winter weather events (when prices spike). It received a weighted-average price of $20.52/MWh — 75% of the simple average of $27.44/MWh. By contrast, solar received only $7.11/MWh — just **23%** of SP15's simple average of $31.00/MWh.

**4. Bottom line on a per-MWh basis**: Every MWh of ERCOT West wind earned **$20.52**, while every MWh of CAISO SP15 solar earned only **$7.11** — a ~2.9× advantage for wind even before the capacity factor difference.

### Final Answer

A wind asset in **ERCOT West** would make you **~$76,644/MW-year** in real-time merchant revenue, versus **~$13,445/MW-year** for an equal-capacity solar asset in **CAISO SP15** — the wind asset wins by roughly **5.7×** on a per-MW-of-capacity basis under these assumptions.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import json

# ===========================================================
# 1. ERCOT West Wind Asset Revenue
# ===========================================================
# Load ERCOT West hourly RT LMPs
ercot_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260618_205449.csv')
ercot_lmp['hour'] = pd.to_datetime(ercot_lmp['hour'])
# ERCOT is in US/Central time
ercot_lmp = ercot_lmp.sort_values('hour')

print("ERCOT data range:", ercot_lmp['hour'].min(), "to", ercot_lmp['hour'].max())
print("ERCOT row count:", len(ercot_lmp))

# Load wind profile (UTC timestamps)
wind_df = pd.read_csv('/work/wind_profile_20260618_205532.csv')
wind_timestamps = []
wind_values = []
for _, row in wind_df.iterrows():
    ts_ms = row['timestamp']
    val_dict = json.loads(row['value'].replace("'", '"'))
    wind_timestamps.append(pd.Timestamp(ts_ms, unit='ms'))
    wind_values.append(val_dict['electricity'])

wind_profile = pd.DataFrame({'utc_hour': wind_timestamps, 'cf': wind_values})
wind_profile['utc_hour'] = wind_profile['utc_hour'].dt.floor('h')

# Convert wind UTC to Central time to match ERCOT timestamps
# ERCOT timestamps are local (CST/CDT)
wind_profile['local_hour'] = wind_profile['utc_hour'].dt.tz_localize('UTC').dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
wind_hourly = wind_profile.groupby('local_hour')['cf'].mean().reset_index()

print("\nWind data range:", wind_hourly['local_hour'].min(), "to", wind_hourly['local_hour'].max())

# Merge ERCOT LMP with wind profile on local hour
merged_wind = pd.merge(ercot_lmp, wind_hourly, left_on='hour', right_on='local_hour', how='inner')
print(f"Merged wind rows: {len(merged_wind)}")

# Calculate hourly revenue per MW of capacity
merged_wind['revenue_per_mwh'] = merged_wind['avg_lmp'] * merged_wind['cf']
total_wind_revenue_per_mw = merged_wind['revenue_per_mwh'].sum()
avg_wind_lmp = merged_wind['avg_lmp'].mean()
weighted_avg_wind_price = merged_wind['revenue_per_mwh'].sum() / merged_wind['cf'].sum()

print(f"\n=== ERCOT West Wind Results ===")
print(f"Annual capacity factor: {merged_wind['cf'].mean():.4f}")
print(f"Avg RT LMP at West hub: ${avg_wind_lmp:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Revenue-weighted avg price: ${weighted_avg_wind_price:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Total revenue per MW of capacity: ${total_wind_revenue_per_mw:,.2f}")

steps.content:
Now I have all the data needed. Here is the complete analysis.

---

## Result: The ERCOT West wind asset generates **~5.7× more merchant revenue** than the CAISO SP15 solar asset

### Data Sources & Methodology

| Component | Data Source | Details |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **ERCOT West RT LMP** | ERCOT database (`ercot_lmp` table) | Real-time LMPs at West Hub, hourly, 2024 (8,783 hours) |
| **CAISO SP15 RT LMP** | GridStatus `caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min` | Real-time 15-min LMPs at `TH_SP15_GEN-APND` trading hub, 2024 (35,129 intervals → hourly averaged) |
| **Wind Profile** | Renewables.ninja | McCamey, TX (31.13°N, -102.22°W), Vestas V90 2000, 80m hub height, 2024 |
| **Solar Profile** | Renewables.ninja | Palm Springs, CA (33.82°N, -116.54°W), fixed 35° tilt, 180° azimuth, 10% system losses, 2024 |

### Revenue Calculation

Merchant revenue = **Hourly LMP × Hourly Generation (CF)**, summed over all 8,784 hours of 2024, no curtailment assumed.

| Metric | **Wind (ERCOT West)** | **Solar (CAISO SP15)** |
|--------|:---------------------:|:----------------------:|
| Annual Capacity Factor | **42.6%** | **21.5%** |
| Total Generation (MWh/MW) | 3,735 | 1,891 |
| Simple Avg RT LMP | **$27.44/MWh** | **$31.00/MWh** |
| **Revenue-Weighted Avg LMP** | **$20.52/MWh** | **$7.11/MWh** |
| **Total Merchant Revenue/MW-year** | **$76,644** | **$13,445** |

### Why the Wind Asset Wins by Such a Large Margin

**1. Higher capacity factor (42.6% vs 21.5%)** — West Texas wind produces ~2× more MWh per MW of capacity than South California solar.

**2. Severe solar price cannibalization in SP15** — The CAISO "duck curve" depresses RT prices precisely when solar generates. During UTC hours 16–23 (roughly California's peak sunlight), the average SP15 RT LMP is just **$8.73/MWh** (compared to an all-hours average of $31.00/MWh). Over 1,183 hours in 2024, SP15 prices went **negative** while the solar asset was generating, destroying ~$16,258/MW in revenue.

**3. Wind captures higher-priced hours** — ERCOT West wind tends to blow more at night (when prices are moderate) and during winter weather events (when prices spike). It received a weighted-average price of $20.52/MWh — 75% of the simple average of $27.44/MWh. By contrast, solar received only $7.11/MWh — just **23%** of SP15's simple average of $31.00/MWh.

**4. Bottom line on a per-MWh basis**: Every MWh of ERCOT West wind earned **$20.52**, while every MWh of CAISO SP15 solar earned only **$7.11** — a ~2.9× advantage for wind even before the capacity factor difference.

### Final Answer

A wind asset in **ERCOT West** would make you **~$76,644/MW-year** in real-time merchant revenue, versus **~$13,445/MW-year** for an equal-capacity solar asset in **CAISO SP15** — the wind asset wins by roughly **5.7×** on a per-MW-of-capacity basis under these assumptions.
